title: Vacancies
text: ReDiLEEP will recruit 13 doctoral candidates into linked response diversity projects.

title: Vacancies
text:
Recruitment of Doctoral Candidates
Recruitment of doctoral candidates will take place during the second half of 2026. General recruitment information, including general eligibility and employment conditions are here:
Read the general recruitment information

title: Vacancies
text:
Planned Doctoral Projects
DC1, LiU: How structural patterns of species interactions affect response diversity
Host: LiU; PhD-awarding entity: LiU; planned start month: 5.
Main supervisor: Anna Eklöf. Co-supervisor(s): György Barabas.
DC2, LiU: Using soundscapes to reveal how species interaction networks shape response diversity
Host: LiU; PhD-awarding entity: LiU; planned start month: 5.
Main supervisor: Anna Eklöf. Co-supervisor(s): Samuel Ross; Sara Keen.
DC3, UOL: The effect and fate of response diversity under environmental change
Host: UOL; PhD-awarding entity: UOL; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Helmut Hillebrand. Co-supervisor(s): Maren Striebel.
DC4, CarU: How can we measure response diversity at scale across taxa?
Host: CarU; PhD-awarding entity: CarU; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Fredric Windsor. Co-supervisor(s): Samuel Ross.
DC5, CarU: Do biotic interactions homogenise responses and reduce response diversity?
Host: CarU; PhD-awarding entity: CarU; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Fredric Windsor. Co-supervisor(s): Giulia Ghedini; Tad Dallas.
DC6, UNamur: A general theory of response diversity effects on stability and coexistence
Host: UNamur; PhD-awarding entity: UNamur; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Frederik De Laender. Co-supervisor(s): Mike Fowler; Giulia Ghedini.
DC7, UNamur: Mean response and response diversity predict impact across environmental contexts
Host: UNamur; PhD-awarding entity: UNamur; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Frederik De Laender. Co-supervisor(s): Owen Petchey.
DC8, CSIC: Disruption of compensatory dynamics under extreme climatic events
Host: CSIC; PhD-awarding entity: Universitat de València; planned start month: 3.
Main supervisor: Francesco de Bello. Co-supervisor(s): Alicia Montesinos.
DC9, UH: Boreal forest and tundra plant communities under environmental change
Host: UH; PhD-awarding entity: UH; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Elina Kaarlejärvi. Co-supervisor(s): Laura Antão.
DC10, SwanU: Restoration of co-invading alien plant species
Host: SwanU; PhD-awarding entity: SwanU; planned start month: 1.
Main supervisor: Mike Fowler. Co-supervisor(s): Dan Eastwood, Dan Jones.
DC11, UZH: Integrating response diversity into policy
Host: UZH; PhD-awarding entity: UZH; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Owen Petchey. Co-supervisor(s): Maria Santos; Cornelia Krug; Ute Jacob.
DC12, UZH: Opportunities and barriers for response-diversity-enabled policy
Host: UZH; PhD-awarding entity: UZH; planned start month: 4.
Main supervisor: Maria Santos. Co-supervisor(s): Owen Petchey; Cornelia Krug; Ute Jacob.
DC13, Monash: Physiological basis and consequences of response diversity
Host: Monash; PhD-awarding entity: Monash; planned start month: 6.
Main supervisor: Giulia Ghedini. Co-supervisor(s): Frederik De Laender; Mike Fowler; Helmut Hillebrand.

title: Training
text: The ReDiLEEP training programme will combine scientific depth with the professional skills needed for collaborative, reproducible, and high-impact response diversity research.

title: Training
text:
Kickoff meeting
Introductory meeting | Month 6 | Lead: LiU
Introduces training and research objectives develops Personal Career Development Plans and establishes cohort links.

title: Training
section: State-of-the-art tools for data management and analysis of response diversity
text:
State-of-the-art tools for data management and analysis of response diversity
Training school | Month 10 | Lead: SwanU | 1.5 ECTS
Covers project and data management version control Tidyverse machine learning and visualisation.

title: Training
text:
Writing science well
Online training school | Month 11 | Lead: OIST | 1.0 ECTS
Builds scientific writing habits including abstracts IMRaD collaborative writing open access publishing and ethical AI use.

title: Training
text:
Planning and conducting quantitative syntheses
Training school | Month 18 | Lead: UOL | 1.5 ECTS
Teaches quantitative synthesis and meta-analysis workflows using response diversity studies.

title: Training
text:
Hackathon on analysis and visualisation
Training school | Month 22 | Lead: UZH | 1.5 ECTS
Combines teamwork data analysis and visualisation through an intensive scientific hackathon.

title: Training
text:
Evidence-based environmental policy making
Online training school | Month 24 | Lead: BioAgora | 1.0 ECTS
Uses evidence-based policy training and ReDiLEEP cases to teach science-policy-industry communication.

title: Training
section: Networking web-presence and virtual career fair
text:
Networking web-presence and virtual career fair
Online training school | Month 28 | Lead: CSIC | 1.0 ECTS
Develops professional presence CVs networking skills and employer-facing presentation through a virtual career fair.

title: Training
text:
Collaborative writing retreat
Training school | Month 32 | Lead: OIST | 1.5 ECTS
Supports papers and thesis chapters through structured writing goals peer feedback and accountability partnerships.

title: Training
section: Dissemination and outreach of response diversity research
text:
Dissemination and outreach of response diversity research
Online training school | Month 34 | Lead: UH | 1.0 ECTS
Trains communication for scientists stakeholders policymakers and the public including policy briefs and media outputs.

title: Training
text:
Final meeting and Research Showcase
Final meeting | Month 42 | Lead: LiU
Presents research in Brussels and supports meetings with policymakers and stakeholders.

title: General Recruitment Information
text: General information for prospective ReDiLEEP doctoral candidate applicants.

title: General Recruitment Information
text:
Degrees, Funding, And Secondments
Each doctoral candidate will be enrolled for a single doctoral degree awarded by the main supervisor’s institution or the relevant local PhD-awarding institution. ReDiLEEP is not a joint-doctorate programme.
All doctoral projects include mandatory secondments. These secondments are an important part of the training programme and will give candidates experience in other countries, institutions, sectors, and research environments.
Funding and contract duration will be between 36 and 48 months depending on the local situation, doctoral programme structure, and host-institution arrangements. The MSCA-funded employment period is normally 36 months, with any additional local support described in the final vacancy advertisement.
ReDiLEEP will offer very competitive salary rates under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions Doctoral Network framework.

title: General Recruitment Information
General Recruitment Information
General Recruitment Information
text:
Benefits And Salary
We offer:

a comprehensive, interactive, and international doctoral training programme;
a dedicated team of experts from European partner institutions and associated international non-academic organisations;
a Personal Career Development Plan (PCDP) to prepare doctoral candidates for their future careers;
local individual training at universities in accordance with the PCDP;
an attractive remuneration package in accordance with MSCA Doctoral Network regulations.

All doctoral candidates will also benefit from professional development opportunities, including secondments, network training modules, transferable-skills courses, workshops, conferences, and public-engagement activities.
Successful candidates will normally receive a full-time employment contract for 36 months under Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions regulations for Doctoral Candidates. Local arrangements may provide support beyond this period where needed for the local doctoral programme.
The salary consists of a monthly living allowance of EUR 4,010 gross, a monthly mobility allowance of EUR 710, and, if applicable, a monthly family allowance of EUR 660. A long-term leave allowance and a special-needs allowance are offered where applicable. Taxation and social contributions, including pension contributions, will be deducted according to national and institutional regulations.
The country correction coefficient applies to the living allowance to support equal treatment and purchasing-power parity. Salary rules and the final monthly amount will vary depending on the recruiting university and country.

title: General Recruitment Information
Research
text: ReDiLEEP research will address how response diversity contributes to ecological stability, resilience, functioning, sustainability, and restoration.

title: Research
text:
Research Structure
The research is composed of three interlinked work packages (WPs) and 13 doctoral projects (DCs). The WPs are designed to address the overarching research questions of the network, while the DCs provide focused research on specific aspects of response diversity. The WPs and DCs are interconnected, with each DC contributing to one or more WPs.

title: Research
text:
Research Work Packages

WP1: Measuring response diversity
Lead: CarU
Develop robust scalable methods for accurately measuring response diversity across ecosystems using non-invasive monitoring experiments theory synthesis and policy analysis.
Task 1.1 non-invasive spatial and temporal monitoring; Task 1.2 experimental setups and analysis pipelines; Task 1.3 interdisciplinary theoretical and analytical frameworks.


WP2: The significance of response diversity for stability
Lead: UNamur
Investigate how response diversity enhances ecological stability across ecosystems and contributes to ecosystem function restoration and sustainability policy.
Task 2.1 mechanisms linking RD and stability; Task 2.2 relative importance of RD compared with other drivers; Task 2.3 practical applications for restoration and socio-ecological sustainability.


WP3: The fate of response diversity under environmental change
Lead: UOL
Investigate how intrinsic drivers such as biotic interactions and extrinsic drivers such as environmental and anthropogenic disturbances shape response diversity.
Task 3.1 biotic interactions and network structure; Task 3.2 chronic press disturbances; Task 3.3 intense pulse disturbances.

title: Research
text:
Doctoral Projects
DC1, LiU: How structural patterns of species interactions affect response diversity
Explores how ecological network structure and response diversity groups affect ecosystem resilience.
Focus: Community detection; ecological network simulations; generated and empirical network data; interactive visualisation.
DC2, LiU: Using soundscapes to reveal how species interaction networks shape response diversity
Uses acoustic monitoring to infer species interactions and test how biotic and abiotic drivers shape response diversity.
Focus: AI pipelines for acoustic recordings; interaction networks; urbanisation gradients; typhoon disturbance data from OIST.
DC3, UOL: The effect and fate of response diversity under environmental change
Tests how phytoplankton response diversity stabilises biomass production and changes under press and pulse warming.
Focus: Thermal reaction norms; metacommunity experiments; heatwaves; legacy effects in plankton communities.
DC4, CarU: How can we measure response diversity at scale across taxa?
Develops field technologies and methods for monitoring response diversity across taxa in managed and unmanaged ecosystems.
Focus: Camera and acoustic sensor arrays; disturbance monitoring; restoration monitoring; comparison with biodiversity credit metrics.
DC5, CarU: Do biotic interactions homogenise responses and reduce response diversity?
Tests how facilitation competition predation and parasitism shape organismal responses and response diversity in freshwater communities.
Focus: Microcosm and mesocosm experiments; multi-species assemblages; temperature treatments; direct and indirect interactions.
DC6, UNamur: A general theory of response diversity effects on stability and coexistence
Develops theory linking response diversity mean response temporal stability and coexistence across environmental drivers.
Focus: Community model structures; parameter databases; Lotka-Volterra and consumer-resource models; simulations across dynamical regimes.
DC7, UNamur: Mean response and response diversity predict impact across environmental contexts
Tests whether response diversity and mean response predict ecological impacts across drivers timescales and ecosystem types.
Focus: Single-species bioassays; algae and protist microcosms; pulse and press disturbances; statistical models.
DC8, CSIC: Disruption of compensatory dynamics under extreme climatic events
Assesses how extreme climatic events disrupt compensatory dynamics and vegetation stability across scales.
Focus: Long-term vegetation and trait data; phenology and abundance; climatic extremes; compensatory dynamics.
DC9, UH: Boreal forest and tundra plant communities under environmental change
Studies response diversity and stability in boreal forest and tundra plant communities along environmental gradients.
Focus: Functional traits; tundra and boreal field systems; remote sensing; environmental change gradients.
DC10, SwanU: Restoration following co-invasion by alien plant species
Investigates how response diversity among co-invading alien plants affects persistence restoration and management outcomes.
Focus: Greenhouse and field experiments; co-invasion dynamics; contaminated post-industrial landscapes; restoration applications.
DC11, UZH: Integrating response diversity into policy
Analyses how response diversity concepts and indicators can be integrated into policy and monitoring frameworks.
Focus: Policy document analysis; biodiversity monitoring; nature restoration policy; policy briefs.
DC12, UZH: Opportunities and barriers for response-diversity-enabled policy
Explores trade-offs barriers and opportunities for using response diversity in sustainability policy and decision-making.
Focus: Agent-based models; socio-economic trade-offs; corporate sustainability reporting; policy implementation barriers.
DC13, Monash: Physiological basis and consequences of response diversity
Tests how physiological responses underpin response diversity and ecological stability in marine phytoplankton.
Focus: Phytoplankton experiments; thermal physiology; community assembly; response and effect traits; mechanistic models.

title: About
text: ReDiLEEP is a funded MSCA Doctoral Network designed to advance response diversity science and translate it into ecosystem preservation, restoration, and policy.

title: About
text:
Aim
The network will train doctoral researchers to work across disciplinary and sector boundaries, with a focus on how response diversity supports socio-ecological resilience under environmental stress. The programme combines 13 linked research projects, network-wide schools, secondments, and shared supervision.

title: About
text:
Approach
ReDiLEEP links field evidence, experimental systems, quantitative modelling, synthesis, and policy engagement. The network is built to help doctoral researchers move confidently between data, theory, communication, and real-world application.

title: About
text:
Objectives

Identify and elucidate mechanisms through which response diversity contributes to socio-ecological resilience under different environmental stressors.
Develop and validate a reproducible standardised toolkit of metrics and empirical methods for quantifying response diversity across ecosystems and spatiotemporal scales.
Co-design practical frameworks for integrating response diversity into conservation restoration and policy decision-making across diverse socio-ecological contexts.

title: About
text:
Work Packages

WP1: Measuring response diversity
Develop robust scalable methods for accurately measuring response diversity across ecosystems using non-invasive monitoring experiments theory synthesis and policy analysis.
Lead: CarU | Months: 1-40 | Doctoral projects: DC2; DC4; DC5; DC6; DC8; DC9; DC10; DC11; DC12; DC13


WP2: The significance of response diversity for stability
Investigate how response diversity enhances ecological stability across ecosystems and contributes to ecosystem function restoration and sustainability policy.
Lead: UNamur | Months: 1-40 | Doctoral projects: DC1; DC3; DC6; DC7; DC8; DC9; DC10; DC11; DC12; DC13


WP3: The fate of response diversity under environmental change
Investigate how intrinsic drivers such as biotic interactions and extrinsic drivers such as environmental and anthropogenic disturbances shape response diversity.
Lead: UOL | Months: 1-40 | Doctoral projects: DC1; DC2; DC3; DC5; DC7; DC10; DC13


WP4: Training
Provide interdisciplinary training in research transferable skills and professional development for careers in academia industry and policymaking.
Lead: UH | Months: 1-40 | Doctoral projects: All


WP5: Communication dissemination and exploitation
Make researchers’ results available to scientific communities industry companies and the public while promoting knowledge on response diversity.
Lead: LiU | Months: 1-40 | Doctoral projects: All


WP6: Project management
Manage recruitment research training finances legal requirements communication reporting and coherent delivery of all project objectives.
Lead: LiU | Months: 1-40 | Doctoral projects: -

title: ReDiLEEP Doctoral Network Funded
text:
ReDiLEEP has been selected for funding as a Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions Doctoral Network.
The network will train doctoral candidates to advance response diversity science and apply it to ecosystem preservation, restoration, biodiversity monitoring, and policy.

title: Contact
text:
Contact
Please address inquiries to Anna Eklöf at Linköping University.

title: ReDiLEEP
text:
MSCA Doctoral Network



Using science-driven response diversity knowledge to leverage efficient ecosystem preservation and restoration.

Explore the network Doctoral opportunities

title: ReDiLEEP
section: A European doctoral network for response diversity
text:
A European doctoral network for response diversity
ReDiLEEP brings together universities, research institutes, policy bodies, NGOs, and industry partners to train doctoral candidates in the science and application of response diversity for resilient ecosystems.
Research Develop and validate a reproducible standardised toolkit of metrics and empirical methods for quantifying response diversity across ecosystems and spatiotemporal scales.
Training Foster interdisciplinary and intersectoral collaboration to close gaps between ecological research policymaking and practical implementation.
Impact Co-design practical frameworks for integrating response diversity into conservation restoration and policy decision-making across diverse socio-ecological contexts.

title: ReDiLEEP
text:
Built Around Doctoral Researchers
The network will recruit doctoral candidates into linked projects hosted across the consortium. Each candidate will receive advanced disciplinary training, secondments, supervision across sectors, and a shared cohort experience.
View planned vacancies

title: ReDiLEEP
text:
Funding Note
ReDiLEEP stands for “Using science-driven Response Diversity knowledge to Leverage Efficient Ecosystem Preservation and restoration.” Add the confirmed grant agreement number and official EU acknowledgement wording when available.
